answer:
I think it's really interesting to consider whether anyone can truly act selflessly without any thought for themselves. You know, I believe that while some people may appear to do things purely for others, there's often an underlying motivation at play. For instance, I remember when I volunteered at a local shelter. At first, I thought I was just helping people in need, which felt good. But, actually, I found that I gained a lot from those experiences too—like a sense of community and purpose, right? 

I mean, it's kind of like this cycle: you help others, and in turn, you feel fulfilled. That's not to say that people don't strive to help honestly, but I think that even small acts of kindness can come with personal benefits, whether it's gaining new experiences or feeling good about oneself. 

Another example is when my friends and I organized a fundraiser. We were really motivated to help raise money for a cause we believed in, but we also enjoyed the camaraderie and fun we had during the planning process. It's hard to separate the altruism from the personal joy derived from those actions, you know?

So, overall, while I do think some people genuinely try to put others first, I believe there's always some level of personal gain involved, even if it's just emotional satisfaction or a sense of achievement. It's part of being human, I guess!
title: Can anyone do things only to help others without thinking of themselves? Why do you think this?
